subUnits.title: 4.1 The Cell as a Living Unit
notes: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms. It is the smallest unit that can carry out all life processes.
The cell theory is a fundamental concept in biology that states:
1. All living organisms are made of one or more cells
2. The cell is the basic unit of structure and function in living things
3. All cells arise from pre-existing cells through cell division
• Robert Hooke (1665): First observed cells in cork
• Matthias Schleiden (1838): All plants made of cells
• Theodor Schwann (1839): All animals made of cells
• Rudolf Virchow (1855): All cells come from pre-existing cells
Forms in Which Living Things Exist:
Living organisms can exist in different forms:
• Carry out all life processes in one cell
• Nigerian context: Protozoa in pond water; bacteria in soil
• Group of similar cells living together
• Cells are attached but can survive independently if separated
• Show division of labor but limited
• Nigerian context: Volvox in stagnant water; Spirogyra in ponds
• Cells arranged in long chains or filaments
• Cells are attached end to end
• Nigerian context: Spirogyra forming green mats in water bodies
• High degree of specialization and organization
• Nigerian context: All crops, livestock, and humans
A typical cell contains various structures (organelles) that perform specific functions:
• Structure: Phospholipid bilayer with embedded proteins
- Controls what enters and exits the cell
• Present in: Both plant and animal cells
• Structure: Gel-like substance filling the cell
• Present in: Both plant and animal cells
- Site of DNA replication and transcription
• Present in: Both plant and animal cells (eukaryotes)
• Present in: Both plant and animal cells
- Rough ER: With ribosomes; protein synthesis
- Smooth ER: Without ribosomes; lipid synthesis, detoxification
• Present in: Both plant and animal cells
• Present in: Both plant and animal cells
• Present in: Both plant and animal cells
- Convert light energy to chemical energy
• Structure: Rigid layer outside cell membrane
• Present in: Plant cells only (also in fungi and bacteria)
Differences Between Plant and Animal Cells:
Similarities Between Plant and Animal Cells:
• Teacher provides prepared slides of Paramecium or Euglena, Volvox and Spirogyra
• Students observe the slides under the microscope and record their observations
• Discussion: What characteristics do these organisms share? How do they differ?
• Teacher mounts slides of plant and animal cells for students to observe
• Students draw, label, and note differences and similarities
• Discussion: Why do plant and animal cells have different structures?
• 'All cells look the same': Cells vary greatly in size, shape, and structure
• 'Plant cells are just animal cells with a wall': Plant and animal cells have fundamental differences
• 'Cell theory means cells are simple': Cells are highly complex, with intricate organization
• 'Only animal cells have nuclei': Both plant and animal cells (eukaryotes) have nuclei
• Understanding cell structure helps in agriculture (crop improvement)
• Knowledge of cells is essential for medicine and health
• Cell biology forms foundation for biotechnology applications
• Microscopy skills important for laboratory work in Nigerian schools
• The cell is the basic unit of life
• Living things exist as independent organisms, colonies, filaments, or multicellular organisms
• Plant and animal cells share common features but have distinct differences
• Understanding cell structure is foundational for all biological studies
• In Nigeria, cell biology knowledge supports agriculture, health, and scientific advancement

Labeled diagram of a typical animal cell showing centrioles, lysosomes

Nigerian examples: Cassava leaf cell, human cheek cell under microscope
subUnits.youtubeQuery: Cell structure plant animal cells biology SSS1 site:youtube.com

opts: Only plants are made of cells
All living things are composed of cells, the cell is the basic unit of life, and cells come from pre-existing cells
quiz.explanation: Cell theory has three core principles that apply to all living organisms, unifying biology.

quiz.q: A key difference between plant and animal cells is that plant cells have:
quiz.explanation: Plant cells have cell walls made of cellulose and chloroplasts for photosynthesis, which animal cells lack.

quiz.q: The organelle responsible for photosynthesis in plant cells is the:
quiz.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and other pigments that capture light energy for photosynthesis.

quiz.q: An example of a colonial organism is:
quiz.explanation: Volvox is a colonial green alga where cells live together but can survive independently if separated.

quiz.q: The powerhouse of the cell is the:
quiz.explanation: Mitochondria produce ATP through cellular respiration, providing energy for cellular activities.

quiz.q: A structure found in animal cells but not in plant cells is:
quiz.explanation: Centrioles are involved in cell division and are present in animal cells but absent in most plant cells.

quiz.q: The function of the cell membrane is to:
opts: Control what enters and exits the cell
quiz.explanation: The cell membrane is selectively permeable, regulating the movement of substances in and out of the cell.
Spirogyra is a filamentous green alga with cells arranged in long chains.

quiz.q: The large central vacuole in plant cells functions to:
opts: Store water and maintain turgor pressure
quiz.explanation: The large central vacuole stores water, nutrients, and wastes, and helps maintain turgor pressure for plant support.

quiz.q: Understanding cell structure is important for Nigerian agriculture because it:
quiz.explanation: Knowledge of cell biology helps in crop improvement, genetic engineering, and agricultural biotechnology applications.
